beats.narration_text: The tiering fix stuck with me — suspicious precision is the tell, and truth is the costume the lie wears, braided with real mechanics like waterfall releases and Release Radar's Friday cadence. Help me build the filter: (1) write the skill file that sorts sources into tier one cite-freely, tier two cite-with-name-flagged, tier three never-as-fact; (2) add the tripwire that flags a term belonging to a different platform instead of repeating it; (3) show me how my scheduled research bot applies this before it hands me a brief.

props.command: The tiering fix stuck with me — suspicious precision is the tell, and truth is the costume the lie wears, braided with real mechanics like waterfall releases and Release Radar's Friday cadence. Help me build the filter: (1) write the skill file that sorts sources into tier one cite-freely, tier two cite-with-name-flagged, tier three never-as-fact; (2) add the tripwire that flags a term belonging to a different platform instead of repeating it; (3) show me how my scheduled research bot applies this before it hands me a brief.
output: Andromeda: real — at Meta, not Spotify
the fix: tier your sources before you research

beats.narration_text: The exhibit. Precise-sounding thresholds. A proprietary-sounding engine name. Formula tables with suspicious precision. That precision is the tell — real platforms don't publish their internals, so anyone quoting exact formulas is either leaking or inventing. They're not leaking.

Why does this work on smart people? Because it launders. One farm invents it, three blogs restate it, and by the fourth telling it arrives as common knowledge. You're busy. You skim. And your scheduled research bot — episode two — will ingest it and repeat it to you as fact, unless you teach it not to. The scammer is counting on exactly that.

And here's what makes these documents dangerous rather than just wrong: they're not all false. The same guide carries real, documented mechanics — waterfall releases, Release Radar's Friday cadence, the thousand-stream royalty threshold — braided with invented ones. The truth is the costume the lie wears. So the fix can't be 'ignore it all.' The fix is tiering.

props.sparkLine: Not everything in it is false.

beats.narration_text: The filter, as a skill file your bot obeys. Tier one, cite freely: Spotify for Artists documentation, engineering blogs, filings, peer review. Tier two, cite with a name attached and flagged as industry claim: agencies with real case numbers. Tier three, never as fact: unsigned posts, invented terminology, suspicious precision. And one tripwire: a term that belongs to a different platform gets flagged, not repeated.

This is the house rule, and now it's yours: no source, no verdict. Not for the guides you read. Not for the briefs your bot writes you. Not for the promises a promo service makes about what the algorithm rewards. The person asking you for three hundred dollars can always cite their source. Watch what happens when you ask.

Let's recap with Claude. The grade, both directions again. The guide: part documented, part invented, all confident. Your defense: a filter your research bot applies before the brief ever reaches you. They aimed a content farm at your hope. You aim a source-tier gate back.

artifactLines: 'Spotify Andromeda': the name is real — at Meta. The Spotify version is a content-farm costume.
The tell: suspicious precision — exact formulas platforms never publish.
The danger: real mechanics (Release Radar Fridays, the 1,000-stream threshold) braided with invented ones.
The fix: the source-tier skill file — tier 1 docs cite freely, tier 3 never as fact, borrowed terms flagged. No source, no verdict.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this, and then feed it the last 'how the algorithm works' guide someone sent you. The re-grade it hands back is this episode, running in your corner, forever.

props.command: Add this rule to my research project: apply source tiering to all platform/algorithm research. Tier 1 (cite freely): Spotify for Artists docs, official engineering blogs, SEC filings, peer-reviewed papers. Tier 2 (cite with named source, flagged as industry claim): named agencies with real case numbers. Tier 3 (never as fact): unsigned SEO posts, invented proprietary terminology, exact-formula claims. Flag any term borrowed from another platform. Then re-grade the last algorithm guide I saved.
